walkthrough.title: Sort the Tile Stacks
walkthrough.text: Let's get Suburbia set up. First, find all the building tiles. They have letters on the back: A, B, and C. Sort them into three face-down stacks by letter. Each stack should have the same number of tiles. For a 2-player game, each stack has 18 tiles. For 3 players, each stack has 25 tiles. For 4 players, each stack has 32 tiles. Put any unused tiles back in the box without looking at them. Keep these stacks face down near the center of the table.

walkthrough.title: Add the 1 More Round Tile
walkthrough.text: Now find the special '1 More Round' tile. This is the timer that ends the game. Shuffle it into the C stack — not the A or B stack. This means you will play through all of the A tiles, then all of the B tiles, and then partway through the C tiles before this tile shows up and triggers the final round. Give the C stack a good shuffle after mixing it in.

walkthrough.title: Place the Stacks and Set Up the Real Estate Market
walkthrough.text: Place the A, B, and C stacks face down in a row near the center of the table. Now find the Real Estate Market board and place it nearby. This is the long board with price spaces printed along the top: $0, $2, $4, $6, and so on. Flip the top 7 tiles from the A stack face up and lay them along the bottom edge of the Real Estate Market, one per price space, starting from the right at $0 and going left. These are the tiles available for purchase this round.

walkthrough.title: Place the Basic Tiles
walkthrough.text: Find the basic tiles: Heavy Factory (yellow, industrial), Community Park (gray, civic), and Suburb (green, residential). Place 4 of each type face up in their own supply stacks near the Real Estate Market. These basic tiles are always available for purchase — they never go in the Real Estate Market itself. They are your fallback options when nothing in the market appeals to you.

walkthrough.title: Place the Supply Board and Money
walkthrough.text: Set out the Supply Board where everyone can reach it. Place all the money nearby — these are the $1, $5, and $10 coins. This is the bank. Everyone will be collecting and paying money throughout the game, so keep it accessible.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Population Board
walkthrough.text: Place the Population Board in the center of the table where everyone can see it. This is the big board that tracks population — which is essentially your victory points in Suburbia. You will notice Red Lines crossing the population track at certain intervals. These are important: every time your population crosses a Red Line going up, your Income and Reputation both drop by 1. This simulates the growing pains of a booming city.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Goals
walkthrough.text: Shuffle all 20 goal tiles face down. Place 1 random goal tile face up per player on the goal spaces along the Real Estate Market. These are the public goals — everyone can see them and compete for them. Put the rest of the goal tiles in a face-down supply for now. These public goals award population bonuses at the end of the game to the single player who best achieves each one.

walkthrough.title: Hand Out Borough Boards
walkthrough.text: Give each player a Borough Board. This is your personal city foundation. Place it in front of you with enough space below it for your borough to grow — you will be placing tiles in a grid extending outward from the board. The Borough Board has tracks for Income and Reputation on it.

walkthrough.title: Set Starting Income and Reputation
walkthrough.text: Each player places their Income Cylinder on the 0 space of the Income track on their Borough Board. Then place your Reputation Cube on the 1 space of the Reputation track. These starting values come from your three starting tiles, which we will place next. Income determines how much money you collect each turn, and Reputation determines how much your population grows each turn.

walkthrough.title: Place Starting Tiles
walkthrough.text: Each player takes one Suburb tile, one Community Park tile, and one Heavy Factory tile from the basic supply and places them below their Borough Board exactly as shown in the rules: the Suburb goes in the center, the Community Park goes adjacent to the Suburb, and the Heavy Factory goes adjacent to the Community Park. These three tiles form your starting borough and establish your initial Income of 0 and Reputation of 1.

walkthrough.title: Place Population Markers
walkthrough.text: Each player places their Population Square on the 2 space of the Population Board. This is your starting population. Your population will grow or shrink throughout the game based on your Reputation. Remember, whoever has the highest population at the end wins.

walkthrough.title: Distribute Starting Money and Investment Markers
walkthrough.text: Each player takes $15 in coins from the supply. This is your starting cash. Also give each player their 3 Investment Markers — these are special tokens you can use later to double the effect of one of your tiles instead of buying a new tile on your turn. Place them next to your Borough Board.

walkthrough.title: Deal Secret Goals
walkthrough.text: Each player draws 2 random goal tiles from the face-down goal supply. Look at both, choose 1 to keep face down as your secret goal, and return the other to the box without showing anyone. Your secret goal gives you a population bonus at the end of the game if you — and only you — have achieved it. If you tie with another player for a secret goal, nobody gets the bonus. So keep it secret and keep an eye on the competition.

walkthrough.title: Choose the Start Player
walkthrough.text: Randomly pick a starting player and give them the Start Player Marker. Play will proceed clockwise from the start player. The start player marker is important because everyone needs to take the same number of turns during the game. You are ready to start building your borough.

walkthrough.title: Turn Overview: Four Actions in Order
walkthrough.text: On your turn in Suburbia, you perform four actions in strict order. First, you take and place a tile (or place an Investment Marker). Second, you collect or pay money based on your Income. Third, you adjust your Population based on your Reputation. Fourth, you refill the Real Estate Market. Every single turn follows this exact sequence. Let's walk through each one.

walkthrough.title: Action 1: Take and Place a Tile
walkthrough.text: This is the heart of every turn. You must either buy a tile from the Real Estate Market, buy a basic tile, take a tile as a lake, or place an Investment Marker. When buying from the Real Estate Market, you pay the price printed on the tile itself plus the additional cost printed above that tile's position on the market. Tiles on the left are more expensive and tiles on the right are cheaper or free. When you buy a basic tile (Suburb, Community Park, or Heavy Factory), you only pay the price on the tile itself — no market surcharge.

walkthrough.title: Placing Your Tile
walkthrough.text: After buying a tile, you must place it adjacent to at least one of your existing tiles. Edges must touch — diagonal does not count. The tile can go around your Borough Board but never on top of it. Placement matters enormously because many tiles have conditional effects based on what is adjacent to them. Think carefully about where each tile goes, because once it is placed, it stays there for the rest of the game.

walkthrough.title: Resolving Tile Effects
walkthrough.text: When you place a tile, resolve its effects in this specific order. First, pay the full cost. Second, apply the immediate effect printed in the upper right of the tile — this adjusts your Income, Reputation, or Population right now. Third, apply the conditional effect at the bottom of the tile — these are ongoing effects like 'for each adjacent residential tile.' Fourth, check if any of your other tiles are triggered by what you just placed. Fifth, check if any opponents' tiles are triggered. This order matters because some tiles interact with each other in chains.

walkthrough.title: The Four Tile Types
walkthrough.text: Every tile belongs to one of four categories, each identified by color. Residential tiles are green — they represent housing and often provide population or reputation. Commercial tiles are blue — they represent businesses and often provide income. Industrial tiles are yellow — they are cheap and boost income, but often hurt reputation. Civic tiles are gray — they provide reputation or special abilities and are generally positive for your borough. Many conditional effects care about the type of adjacent tiles, so knowing the colors is essential.

walkthrough.title: Lakes: Flipping a Tile Face Down
walkthrough.text: Instead of placing a tile face up, you can buy any tile from the Real Estate Market and flip it face down to use as a lake. When you do this, you only pay the market surcharge — not the tile's own cost. Place the face-down tile adjacent to your borough like any other tile. For each adjacent tile of any type (residential, commercial, industrial, or civic), you immediately receive $2 from the supply. Lakes are useful for quick cash and for blocking opponents from getting a tile they need. However, a lake has no ongoing effects and no tile type — it is just a blank space.

walkthrough.title: Investment Markers
walkthrough.text: Instead of buying a tile, you can place one of your 3 Investment Markers on any tile in your borough that does not already have one — including lakes. Pay the original cost of that tile again, then place the Investment Marker on its top-left corner. The Investment Marker doubles the effects of that tile for the rest of the game. For example, a Community Park that gives +1 Reputation per adjacent green tile would now give +2 per adjacent green tile. However, placing an Investment Marker does not re-trigger other tiles' conditional effects. After placing an Investment Marker, you must still discard a tile from the Real Estate Market, paying only the market surcharge.

walkthrough.title: Discarding a Tile After Basic Tiles or Investment Markers
walkthrough.text: Here is a rule that new players often miss. Whenever you buy a basic tile or place an Investment Marker instead of buying from the Real Estate Market, you must also discard one face-up tile from the market. You pay only the market surcharge for the discarded tile — not the tile's own cost. This is important strategically: you might pay extra to discard a tile that would help the player after you.

walkthrough.title: Action 2: Collect or Pay Money (Income)
walkthrough.text: After placing your tile, check your Income Cylinder. If it is on a positive number, collect that much money from the supply. If it is on a negative number, you must pay that amount to the supply. If you cannot afford to pay, pay what you can and then move your Population Square backwards one space per $1 you still owe. Your population can never drop below 0, so if you run out of both money and population, you simply stop. Income ranges from -5 to 15.

walkthrough.title: Action 3: Adjust Population (Reputation)
walkthrough.text: Now check your Reputation Cube. Move your Population Square on the Population Board by the number where your Reputation Cube sits. Positive Reputation means your population goes up; negative means it goes down. If your population would drop below 0, each additional point down costs you $1 instead. If you cannot pay, your population stays at 0. Reputation ranges from -5 to 15.

walkthrough.title: Red Lines: The Growth Penalty
walkthrough.text: This is the mechanic that makes Suburbia tick. The Population Board has Red Lines at certain intervals. Every time your Population Square crosses a Red Line going up, your Income and Reputation are both immediately reduced by 1. This happens in the middle of resolving effects — you do not wait until the end of your turn. If you cross multiple Red Lines in one turn, you take the penalty for each one. This simulates urban growing pains: a booming city costs more to maintain and becomes less attractive. It means you need to build Income and Reputation infrastructure before chasing raw population, or the Red Lines will drag you down.

walkthrough.title: Action 4: Refill the Real Estate Market
walkthrough.text: After adjusting your population, slide all remaining tiles in the Real Estate Market to the right to fill any gaps. Then flip the top tile from the current stack (A first, then B, then C) and place it on the leftmost open space of the market. This means the newest tile always enters at the most expensive position. Over time, tiles slide right toward the cheaper spaces if nobody buys them.

walkthrough.title: Game End: The 1 More Round Tile
walkthrough.text: When the 1 More Round tile is flipped from the C stack, the current round finishes — meaning every player after the current player in turn order completes their turn. Then one additional full round is played, starting with the Start Player. After that final round, the game ends. Everyone should have taken the same total number of turns.

walkthrough.title: Final Scoring: Goals
walkthrough.text: Final scoring has two parts, and you do them in order. First, score the public goals on the Real Estate Market. Each public goal awards a population bonus to whichever single player achieves it. If two or more players tie for a public goal, nobody gets the bonus. Then, each player reveals their secret goal. If you — and only you — achieved your secret goal, you earn its population bonus. Red Lines are ignored during final scoring, so goal bonuses do not trigger Income and Reputation penalties.

walkthrough.title: Final Scoring: Money Conversion and Winning
walkthrough.text: After goals, each player converts their remaining money into population at a rate of 1 population per $5, rounded down. Again, Red Lines are ignored for this conversion. The player with the highest population on the Population Board wins. Tiebreakers are: highest Reputation, then highest Income, then most remaining money. If it is still tied, the designers suggest playing another game of Suburbia to break it.

walkthrough.title: Welcome & Meet the Players
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted first few turns with three example players — Toni, Dakota, and Gage. Each will make different choices so you can see how tile buying, placement, and the economy all work. Follow along even if you have a different player count. Ready to build some boroughs? Let's go.

walkthrough.title: Starting Position Reminder
walkthrough.text: Everyone starts with the same three tiles: a Suburb (green), a Community Park (gray), and a Heavy Factory (yellow). Your Income is at 0, Reputation is at 1, Population is at 2, and you have $15. The Real Estate Market has 7 face-up tiles from the A stack, with prices ranging from $0 on the right to $6 on the left. Remember, you also pay the price printed on the tile itself.

walkthrough.title: Toni's Turn: Buying from the Market
walkthrough.text: Toni goes first. She spots a Parking Lot in the $2 position of the Real Estate Market. The Parking Lot costs $4 on the tile itself, plus $2 from the market position, for a total of $6. Toni pays $6 to the supply and places the Parking Lot adjacent to her Community Park. The Parking Lot is a commercial (blue) tile that provides +1 Income for the tile itself, plus +1 Income for each adjacent civic (gray) or commercial (blue) tile. Since the Community Park is civic, Toni gets +2 Income total. She moves her Income Cylinder from 0 to 2. Nice start.

walkthrough.title: Toni Collects Income and Gains Population
walkthrough.text: Now Toni collects money equal to her Income: $2 from the supply. Then she adjusts her Population by her Reputation, which is still 1. Her Population moves from 2 to 3. Finally, a new tile slides in from the A stack to fill the gap in the market. Toni spent $6 but got $2 back from Income, and her city is already growing. That is a solid first turn.

walkthrough.title: Dakota's Turn: An Income-Focused Play
walkthrough.text: Dakota looks at the market and finds an Office Building on the $0 space — free market surcharge. The tile itself costs $9, so Dakota pays $9 total. She places it adjacent to her Heavy Factory and Community Park. The Office Building gives +1 Income immediately, plus +1 Income for each adjacent commercial (blue) or civic (gray) tile. The adjacent Community Park is gray, so that is +2 Income total. Dakota moves her Income Cylinder from 0 to 2.

walkthrough.title: Why Income Early Is Powerful
walkthrough.text: Notice how both Toni and Dakota immediately went for tiles that boost Income. In Suburbia, early Income is incredibly valuable because you collect it every single turn for the rest of the game. An Income boost on turn 1 is worth roughly 15 to 18 dollars over the course of the game. By contrast, a direct population boost on turn 1 might actually hurt you if it pushes you past a Red Line before your economy is ready.

walkthrough.title: Gage's Turn: The Mistake — Chasing Population Early
walkthrough.text: Gage sees a Homeowner's Association that costs $6 total. It provides +2 Reputation immediately. Reputation means more population each turn, which sounds great. But Gage ignores his Income, which is still sitting at 0. He places the tile, bumps his Reputation to 3, and his population shoots up quickly. By the end of his second turn, he crosses the first Red Line — and both his Income and Reputation drop by 1. His Income is now -1, meaning he has to pay $1 every turn. His Reputation dropped from 3 to 2. Gage is bleeding money and his growth already slowed down.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Build Income Before Reputation
walkthrough.text: The lesson from Gage's mistake is critical: do not chase population early. Red Lines punish fast growth. You need an Income engine running first so you can absorb the penalties. Toni and Dakota built Income to 2 before worrying about Reputation or Population. Gage jumped ahead in population but is now paying for it every turn. In Suburbia, the turtle usually beats the hare.

walkthrough.title: Toni's Second Turn: Thinking About Adjacency
walkthrough.text: Toni wants another commercial tile to take advantage of her Parking Lot's 'for each adjacent blue tile' bonus. She buys a Fast Food Restaurant from the market for $7 total and places it next to her Parking Lot. The Parking Lot now has two adjacent matching tiles (Community Park for gray, Fast Food for blue), bumping Toni's Income up again. But the Fast Food Restaurant has a negative conditional: it reduces Reputation for each adjacent residential (green) tile. Toni placed it away from her Suburb — smart adjacency planning.

walkthrough.title: Gage's Mistake: Ignoring Adjacency
walkthrough.text: Meanwhile, Gage buys a Slaughterhouse — a cheap industrial tile that gives good Income but reduces Reputation for each adjacent residential tile. He places it right next to his Suburb without thinking about the conditional effect. His Reputation drops by 1. Now his Reputation boost from the Homeowner's Association is partially cancelled by the Slaughterhouse penalty. He is paying for tiles that fight each other. If he had placed the Slaughterhouse on the other side of his borough, away from the Suburb, he would have avoided the penalty entirely.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Adjacency Is Everything
walkthrough.text: In Suburbia, where you place a tile matters as much as what tile you buy. Industrial tiles and residential tiles generally do not like being next to each other. Commercial tiles and civic tiles often work well together. Always read the conditional effect at the bottom of every tile before you place it, and think about what you might want to place next to it in future turns. Planning your borough layout two or three tiles ahead is what separates good players from great ones.

walkthrough.title: Dakota's Turn: Using a Lake
walkthrough.text: Dakota wants to deny Gage a tile that would help him recover. She sees a nice Reputation-boosting civic tile in the $0 position that would be perfect for Gage. Dakota decides to take it as a lake. She pays $0 (the market surcharge) and places it face down adjacent to her borough, where it touches three existing tiles. She collects $2 per adjacent tile — that is $6 cash immediately. The tile is gone from the market, Gage cannot get it, and Dakota has extra money to spend next turn. Clever.

walkthrough.title: Gage's Mistake: Forgetting to Discard Strategically
walkthrough.text: On a later turn, Gage decides to buy a basic Suburb instead of a market tile. He forgets that when you buy a basic tile, you must also discard a tile from the market. He could pay $2 to discard a tile that would be perfect for Toni, but instead he grabs the free $0 tile to discard without thinking. On Toni's next turn, she buys that perfect tile for cheap. Always think about who sits after you in turn order when discarding — denying your opponents a key tile can be worth the extra cost.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: The Discard Is a Weapon
walkthrough.text: Every time you buy a basic tile or place an Investment Marker, you get to remove a tile from the market. This is not a throwaway step — it is a strategic weapon. Look at what the player to your left needs. If they are building a commercial engine, discard the commercial tiles. If they are chasing a goal, discard tiles that help them. Sometimes spending $2 to $4 extra on the discard is the best investment you make all game.

walkthrough.title: Mid-Game Check: Red Lines and Economy
walkthrough.text: A few rounds in, let's check the scoreboard. Toni has Income 4 and Reputation 2. She is collecting $4 per turn and gaining 2 population per turn. She has crossed one Red Line but planned for it by building Income first. Dakota has Income 3 and Reputation 3 — balanced and steady. Gage has Income 1 and Reputation 1 — after two Red Line penalties and poor adjacency, his early population lead has evaporated. Toni and Dakota are now overtaking him.

walkthrough.title: Gage's Redemption: Investment Marker
walkthrough.text: Gage decides to use an Investment Marker on his Homeowner's Association, paying its tile cost again. Now its Reputation bonus is doubled. He also strategically discards a tile that would help Toni. Gage is learning to play both sides of the game — building his own borough while disrupting opponents. His Reputation climbs back up and his population starts growing again.

walkthrough.title: Continue Playing
walkthrough.text: Play continues around the table. Each turn follows the same four-step sequence: buy and place a tile, collect or pay Income, adjust Population by Reputation, and refill the market. The A tiles will run out first and you will start flipping B tiles, which are generally more powerful and more expensive. Then come the C tiles. Keep an eye on the goal tiles — both public and secret — and plan your borough layout carefully.

walkthrough.title: Game End and Scoring
walkthrough.text: When the 1 More Round tile flips, finish the current round and play one more full round. Then score public goals — remember, ties mean nobody gets the bonus. Reveal secret goals. Convert leftover money to population at $5 per 1 population. Whoever has the highest population wins.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have seen how to buy tiles, manage adjacency, handle the Income and Reputation economy, deal with Red Lines, use lakes and Investment Markers, and play the discard game. You watched Gage learn from three common mistakes: chasing population too early, ignoring adjacency effects, and wasting the discard step. You are ready to build your own borough.

walkthrough.title: Before You Build
walkthrough.text:
Before you jump into a full game, here are three things that can help.

First — check out General Tips for strategy basics and common mistakes to avoid.

Second — reference the Appendix for a quick guide to tile types and game terms.

And most importantly — ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

summary.title: Gage's Mistake: Forgetting to Discard Strategically
bullets: Buying a basic tile or placing an Investment Marker = must discard a market tile
Gage discards the free tile instead of denying opponents
The next player snags a perfect tile on the cheap

summary.title: Lesson: The Discard Is a Weapon
bullets: Discard step is strategic, not throwaway
Deny the player to your left their ideal tile
Spending $2-$4 extra to discard a key tile is often worth it

summary.title: Mid-Game Check: Red Lines and Economy
bullets: Toni: Income 4, Rep 2 — strong, planned for Red Lines
Dakota: Income 3, Rep 3 — balanced and steady
Gage: Income 1, Rep 1 — early lead evaporated due to Red Line penalties

summary.title: Gage's Redemption: Investment Marker
bullets: Investment Marker doubles a tile's effects permanently
Pay the tile's cost again, then discard a market tile strategically
Gage recovers Reputation while disrupting Toni

summary.title: Continue Playing
bullets: Same 4-step sequence every turn: buy, income, population, refill
A tiles first, then B (stronger), then C (strongest)
Keep goals — both public and secret — in mind at all times

summary.title: Game End and Scoring
bullets: 1 More Round tile: finish round, then play 1 more full round
Score public goals (ties = nobody gets bonus), reveal secret goals
Convert money to population at $5 per 1 population

summary.title: Congratulations
bullets: You've practiced buying, placing, adjacency, economy, lakes, and Investment Markers
Watched Gage learn from 3 mistakes: early population, bad adjacency, wasted discards
Ready to build your own borough

summary.title: Before You Build
bullets: Check out General Tips for strategy basics
Reference the Appendix for tile types and terms
Ask your personal GM Guide anytime — tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before we get into strategy, let's cover the most common mistakes new Suburbia players make. First, chasing population before building Income — Red Lines will destroy your economy. Second, ignoring adjacency when placing tiles — conditional effects are the most powerful part of the game. Third, hoarding money instead of investing it in tiles — cash sitting in your supply does nothing until final scoring, and even then it is only 1 population per $5. Fourth, forgetting that your discard step is a strategic tool, not a chore. And fifth, ignoring goals until the last few turns — by then it is usually too late to pivot. Keep these in mind and you will avoid the biggest traps.

walkthrough.title: Prioritize Income in the Early Game
walkthrough.text: Income is the most important resource in the first third of the game. Every point of Income on turn one is worth $15-$18 over the course of the game because you collect it every single turn. Focus your first two or three tile purchases on commercial and industrial tiles that boost Income. An Income of 3 or 4 by the end of the first few rounds gives you the cash flow to buy premium tiles from the B and C stacks later. Without Income, you will be stuck buying cheap tiles while your opponents snap up the powerful ones.

walkthrough.title: Respect the Red Lines
walkthrough.text: Red Lines are the defining mechanic of Suburbia. Every time your population crosses one going up, your Income and Reputation each drop by 1. This means you need an Income and Reputation buffer before your population starts climbing. A good rule of thumb: try to have your Income at least 2 points higher than you need for expenses before you start boosting Reputation. If your Income is 4 and you cross a Red Line, it drops to 3 — still positive. If your Income is 0 and you cross a Red Line, you are suddenly losing money every turn.

walkthrough.title: Plan Your Borough Layout
walkthrough.text: Adjacency is everything in Suburbia. Before you place a tile, think about not just its current neighbors, but what you might want to place next to it in the future. Keep industrial tiles on one side of your borough and residential tiles on the other — they almost always penalize each other. Group civic and commercial tiles together when their conditional effects synergize. Leave open edges where you plan to expand with matching tile types. Experienced players think at least two or three tiles ahead when choosing placement.

walkthrough.title: Watch the Market and Your Opponents
walkthrough.text: The Real Estate Market is a shared resource. Every tile you take is a tile your opponents cannot have, and every tile you discard is denied to the player after you. Before your turn, scan the market for tiles that help your opponents — especially the player to your left who goes after you. If a tile is incredibly valuable to them, consider taking it as a lake or paying extra to discard it. Similarly, if a tile is mediocre for you but terrible for an opponent, it might be worth buying just to keep it away.

walkthrough.title: Do Not Ignore Goals
walkthrough.text: Goals can swing the game by 10-20 population points. There are public goals on the Real Estate Market that everyone can see, and your secret goal that only you know. Check the public goals at the start of the game and factor them into your tile choices. If a public goal rewards having the most commercial tiles, lean toward commercial tiles when choices are close. For your secret goal, build toward it from the first turn — but do not telegraph it to opponents, or they might tie you and nobody gets the bonus.

walkthrough.title: Use Investment Markers at the Right Time
walkthrough.text: You only get 3 Investment Markers all game, so use them wisely. The best Investment Marker targets are tiles with strong conditional effects that scale with your borough. A tile that gives +1 Income per adjacent commercial tile becomes +2 per adjacent commercial tile when invested. The more neighbors it has (or will have), the bigger the payoff. Invest early on tiles that will compound over many turns. Investing on the last turn is much less valuable because the doubled effect has no time to accumulate.

walkthrough.title: Balance Income, Reputation, and Cash
walkthrough.text: The winning strategy in Suburbia is balance. You need Income to fund tile purchases and survive Red Lines. You need Reputation to grow your population. And you need enough cash on hand to buy premium tiles when they appear. A player with high Income but no Reputation will have money but no population. A player with high Reputation but no Income will grow fast and then crash at every Red Line. Aim for a steady ramp: build Income first, then start adding Reputation once your economy can handle the Red Line penalties.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Don't chase population before building Income — Red Lines will crush you
Don't ignore adjacency — conditional effects are the most powerful mechanic
Don't hoard cash — it only converts at $5 per 1 population at game end
Don't treat the discard step as a throwaway — it is a strategic weapon
Don't ignore goals until the last few turns — build toward them from the start

Build Income buffer before boosting Reputation/Population
Rule of thumb: Income should be 2+ above break-even before growing
Crossing Red Lines with 0 Income = bleeding money every turn

Market awareness wins close games

Check all goals before each purchase decision

Best on tiles with scaling conditional effects and many neighbors

Last-turn investments have minimal impact

summary.title: Balance Income, Reputation, and Cash
bullets: Income funds tiles and absorbs Red Line penalties
Reputation grows population but triggers Red Lines
Cash reserves let you buy premium tiles when they appear
Build Income first, then layer in Reputation once economy can handle penalties

walkthrough.title: The Income Engine Opening
walkthrough.text: The strongest opening in Suburbia is building a robust Income engine in the first four or five turns. Focus on commercial and industrial tiles that provide immediate Income boosts and conditional Income for adjacent matching tiles. An Office Building adjacent to civic tiles, a Parking Lot next to commercial tiles, or even a couple of Heavy Factories grouped together can push your Income to 5 or 6 by the time B tiles start appearing. This cash flow lets you afford premium B and C tiles that other players simply cannot buy.

walkthrough.title: Red Line Management
walkthrough.text: Expert players plan their Red Line crossings. Count the spaces between your current population and the next Red Line. Calculate when your Reputation will push you past it, and make sure your Income can absorb the -1 penalty. The ideal scenario is to cross a Red Line on a turn where you also gain a significant Income boost — the boost offsets the penalty immediately. Some players deliberately keep Reputation low early to avoid Red Lines, then spike Reputation in the mid-to-late game when their Income engine is fully built.

walkthrough.title: Tile Synergy Clusters
walkthrough.text: The most powerful boroughs are built around clusters of synergistic tiles. A cluster of three or four commercial tiles with conditional bonuses for adjacent commercial tiles can generate massive Income. A civic cluster with reputation bonuses per adjacent green or gray tile creates a reputation fortress. The key is identifying which cluster type to commit to early (usually based on what A tiles are available) and then drafting toward it consistently. Do not spread your tile types evenly across your borough — concentrate them for maximum conditional effect stacking.

walkthrough.title: The Lake Strategy
walkthrough.text: Lakes are underappreciated by new players. A strategically placed lake generates $2 per adjacent tile immediately, and it occupies a space without any negative conditional effects. Use lakes to fill gaps in your borough where placing a real tile would trigger penalties — for instance, between your industrial cluster and your residential cluster. Lakes also deny opponents key tiles while putting cash in your pocket. Some players use two or three lakes over the course of a game for both cash generation and denial.

walkthrough.title: Investment Marker Timing
walkthrough.text: Investment Markers are most powerful on tiles with conditional effects that scale with your borough size. The ideal investment target is a tile that gives +1 of something per adjacent matching tile, placed in a spot with three or four matching neighbors. Doubling that to +2 per neighbor for the rest of the game is devastating. Time your Investment Markers for the mid-game, after your borough layout is established but with enough turns remaining for the doubled effect to pay off. Investing on turn 3 with 12 turns left is far more valuable than investing on turn 14 with 2 turns left.

walkthrough.title: Goal-Oriented Building
walkthrough.text: Goals are worth significant population bonuses, and ties cancel them entirely. This creates interesting dynamics. For public goals, watch what your opponents are building and assess whether you can realistically win each goal. If two other players are both building toward the same public goal, they might tie — letting you ignore it. If a goal aligns naturally with your tile strategy, lean into it hard and try to build an insurmountable lead. For your secret goal, you have the advantage of information — nobody else knows what you are targeting, so they cannot deliberately tie you.

walkthrough.title: Denial and Hate-Drafting
walkthrough.text: In Suburbia, every tile you take or discard affects all players. Advanced players track what their opponents need and actively deny it. If the player to your left is building a commercial cluster and a perfect commercial tile is in the market, take it as a lake or pay to discard it. The discard step when buying basic tiles or placing Investment Markers is especially powerful for denial — you get to remove a tile just by paying the market surcharge. In tight games, the player who best manages denial often wins.

walkthrough.title: Late-Game Population Sprint
walkthrough.text: The endgame in Suburbia is a sprint for population. Once the C tiles start appearing, shift your strategy from engine-building to maximizing population output. This is when you want high Reputation — each point of Reputation is worth 1 population per remaining turn. Look for tiles that give large immediate population bonuses or tiles with conditional effects that apply to all boroughs (not just adjacent tiles). The 1 More Round tile gives you one final round — plan your last few purchases to maximize the population boost from that final Reputation collection.

walkthrough.title: Money-to-Population Conversion
walkthrough.text: Do not forget that leftover money converts to population at $5 per 1 population at game end. If your final score is going to be close, having $15-$20 in reserve could mean 3-4 extra population. But this is a last resort — money invested in tiles during the game compounds. A tile that boosts Income by 1 on turn 5 is worth about $12 over the rest of the game, which is 2 population from conversion. But that tile might also have conditional effects, adjacency bonuses, and goal implications worth far more. Only hoard money in the final 2-3 turns when there are no good tile options left.

walkthrough.title: Reading the Stacks
walkthrough.text: Familiarity with the tile distribution across stacks A, B, and C gives you a significant advantage. A tiles tend to be simpler with smaller effects — they are foundation tiles. B tiles introduce stronger conditional effects and interactive icons. C tiles have the most powerful immediate effects and often reference all boroughs, not just adjacent tiles. Knowing what might come from each stack lets you build your borough in anticipation. If you know a powerful civic tile appears in the B stack that gives Reputation per commercial tile in your borough, building a commercial cluster during the A stack phase sets up a huge payoff when that B tile appears.

bullets: First 4-5 turns: focus on Income-boosting commercial and industrial tiles

Target Income 5-6 before B tiles appear

Never cross a Red Line with Income at 0 or below

Plan for the 1 More Round tile — your final Reputation collection matters

bullets: A tiles: simple foundations; B tiles: stronger conditionals; C tiles: powerful all-borough effects
Build your A-stack borough in anticipation of known B and C tiles
Familiarity with tile distribution is a significant competitive edge
Commercial clusters in A phase set up huge B/C civic tile payoffs

entries.definition: Your personal city area. It consists of your Borough Board and all tiles placed below and around it. Each player builds their own borough independently, though some tile effects reference other players' boroughs.

The starting board placed in front of each player. It has tracks for Income and Reputation. Tiles are placed adjacent to and extending out from the Borough Board. The board can be flipped for different orientations without affecting gameplay.

The victory point currency of Suburbia. Tracked on the shared Population Board. Adjusted each turn based on your Reputation value. Highest population at game end wins. Population can never go below 0.
The shared central board that tracks all players' population. Contains Red Lines at intervals that penalize Income and Reputation when crossed upward. Ranges from 0 upward.
Each player's marker on the Population Board, tracking their current population (victory points). Moves up or down each turn based on Reputation.
Tracked on your Borough Board. Determines how much money you collect (positive) or pay (negative) each turn. Range: -5 to 15. Affected by tile effects and Red Line penalties.
The marker placed on your Borough Board's Income track. Moves left (decrease) or right (increase) as tile effects and Red Lines modify your Income. Starts at 0.
Tracked on your Borough Board. Determines how much your population increases (positive) or decreases (negative) each turn. Range: -5 to 15. Affected by tile effects and Red Line penalties.
The marker placed on your Borough Board's Reputation track. Moves left (decrease) or right (increase) as tile effects and Red Lines modify your Reputation. Starts at 1.

Lines on the Population Board that trigger penalties when your Population Square crosses them going up. Each Red Line crossed reduces both your Income and Reputation by 1. Red Lines are ignored during final scoring (goals and money conversion).

The shared market board displaying face-up tiles available for purchase. Each position has a surcharge ($0, $2, $4, $6, etc.) added to the tile's own cost. Tiles slide right to fill gaps, and new tiles enter from the left at the most expensive position.

The additional cost printed above each position on the Real Estate Market. Paid on top of the tile's own cost. Tiles on the left are more expensive; tiles on the right are cheaper or free. Tiles slide right over time, getting cheaper.

Green-colored tiles representing housing. Examples include Suburb, Affordable Housing, Homeowner's Association, and Mansion. Often provide population or reputation bonuses. Frequently penalized by adjacent industrial tiles.

Blue-colored tiles representing businesses. Examples include Parking Lot, Fast Food Restaurant, Office Building, and Shopping Mall. Typically boost Income. Often synergize with adjacent civic and commercial tiles.

Yellow-colored tiles representing factories and industry. Examples include Heavy Factory, Slaughterhouse, and Landfill. Cheap to buy and provide good Income, but often reduce Reputation, especially when adjacent to residential tiles.

Gray-colored tiles representing public buildings and services. Examples include Community Park, School, Library, and Hospital. Generally provide Reputation bonuses and have positive interactions. Rarely have negative conditional effects.

The three tile types always available outside the Real Estate Market: Suburb (green), Community Park (gray), and Heavy Factory (yellow). Purchased at face cost only (no market surcharge). Limited supply of 4 each. Buying a basic tile requires discarding one market tile.
A basic residential (green) tile. Provides +2 Population as an immediate effect. One of the three starting tiles in every player's borough. Always available for purchase from the basic supply.

A basic civic (gray) tile. Provides +1 Reputation for each adjacent residential (green) tile and -1 Reputation for each adjacent industrial (yellow) tile as a conditional effect. One of the three starting tiles. Always available for purchase.

A basic industrial (yellow) tile. Provides +1 Income for each adjacent commercial (blue) or industrial (yellow) tile as a conditional effect. One of the three starting tiles. Always available for purchase. Cheap but can hurt Reputation if near civic or residential tiles.

A face-down tile placed in your borough. Created by buying any market tile and flipping it face down. You pay only the market surcharge, not the tile cost. Provides $2 per adjacent tile immediately upon placement. Has no ongoing effects, no tile type, and no color. Can receive an Investment Marker.

A special tile shuffled into the C stack during setup. When flipped, it signals the game is ending: finish the current round, then play one more full round starting with the Start Player. In the solo game variant, the game ends immediately when this tile appears.

Each player has 3 markers that can be placed on tiles in their borough to double the tile's effects permanently. Costs the tile's original price again. Placing one requires discarding a market tile. Cannot be placed on a tile that already has one. Does not re-trigger other tiles' conditional effects.

The effect printed in the upper right of a tile, resolved immediately when the tile is placed. Typically adjusts Income, Reputation, or Population by a fixed amount. Happens once — when the tile is first placed.

The effect printed at the bottom of a tile, based on a condition such as 'for each adjacent' or 'for every.' Applied when the tile is placed AND whenever the condition changes (e.g., a new matching tile is placed nearby). Ongoing for the rest of the game.

The price printed in the upper left corner of a tile. Paid to the supply when purchasing the tile. When buying from the Real Estate Market, you also pay the market surcharge on top of this. When buying a basic tile, you pay only this cost.

An optional symbol on the right side of some tiles (e.g., office briefcase, restaurant fork). Tiles with matching interactive icons trigger each other's effects. For example, the Business Supply Store gains Income for every tile with an office icon in all boroughs.

Two tiles are adjacent when one straight edge of one tile touches one straight edge of the other. Diagonal does not count. Adjacency matters for conditional effects — most 'for each adjacent' effects only count direct edge-to-edge neighbors.

A conditional effect term. Count the number of tiles directly edge-to-edge next to the tile in question. Only straight edges count — not diagonals. Used on tiles like Community Park ('for each adjacent green tile').

A conditional effect term. Refers to all tiles of a specified type in your own borough, including the tile itself if it matches. For example, a High School provides a bonus for each residential tile in your borough.

A conditional effect term. Refers to all tiles of a specified type in ALL boroughs — yours and all opponents'. Includes the tile itself if it matches the criteria. The broadest scope of any conditional effect.
A conditional effect term. Refers to tiles in every other player's borough, not your own. Used on tiles that interact with or respond to opponents' construction.

When a tile says 'take $2' or similar, you receive that money from the supply both when you place the tile and again whenever a qualifying tile is placed later (by anyone). These are ongoing effects that trigger throughout the game.
The first set of building tiles used in the game. Contains simpler, lower-cost tiles that form the foundation of your borough. All A tiles are used before B tiles enter the market.
The second set of building tiles. Contains more powerful tiles with stronger conditional effects and interactive icons. Enters the market after all A tiles are depleted.

The final set of building tiles, which includes the shuffled-in 1 More Round tile. Contains the most powerful tiles, often with effects referencing all boroughs. The game ends when the 1 More Round tile is flipped from this stack.
Goal tiles placed face up on the Real Estate Market during setup (1 per player). Award population bonuses at game end to the single player who achieves each goal. Ties mean nobody receives the bonus.

Each player's private goal tile, chosen from 2 drawn during setup. Awards a population bonus at game end if you — and only you — achieved it. Kept face down until final scoring. If tied with another player, no one receives the bonus.

Given to the randomly selected first player. Determines turn order (clockwise from the Start Player). Important for the final round triggered by the 1 More Round tile — the Start Player begins the last round.
The central board where money, basic tiles, and game stacks are organized. Houses the bank of $1, $5, and $10 coins.

Where tiles removed from the Real Estate Market go. Tiles are discarded when a player buys a basic tile or places an Investment Marker (they must remove one market tile). Discarded tiles are out of the game.
A commercial (blue) tile that provides +1 Income immediately and +1 Income for each adjacent commercial or civic tile. A strong early-game Income engine tile.
A commercial (blue) tile that provides Income bonuses based on adjacent tiles. A solid tile for commercial clusters. Has an interactive icon that interacts with other parking-related tiles.
A commercial (blue) tile that provides Income but reduces Reputation for each adjacent residential (green) tile. Place it away from your housing area to avoid the penalty.
A residential (green) tile that provides Reputation bonuses based on the number of residential tiles. A strong target for Investment Markers if you are building a residential-heavy borough.

A tile with an effect that scales based on every Airport in all boroughs (not just your own). Each Airport you or opponents build makes every existing Airport more powerful. One of the strongest 'every' effect tiles.
An industrial (yellow) tile that provides good Income at the cost of Reputation per adjacent residential tile. Classic industrial-residential conflict tile — keep it far from your housing.

At game end, after goals are scored, each player converts their remaining money: 1 Population per $5 spent to the supply (rounded down). Red Lines are ignored during this conversion. Leftover money (less than $5) serves as a tiebreaker.

If players are tied in population at game end: 1st tiebreaker is highest Reputation, 2nd is highest Income, 3rd is most remaining money. If still tied, the rulebook humorously suggests playing another game.
